lesson.menu_desc: Cleft sentences + emphatic inversion for persuasion -- 29 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 13 -- Closing Arguments: persuading the court in your summation.
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Luiz Bressane | Aula 13 -- Closing Arguments

listenings.text: Before you deliver your first summation, let me tell you what truly persuades a jury. It is not volume, and it is not theatrics. What wins a closing argument is structure. Begin by reminding the jury of the burden: the prosecution must prove its case beyond a reasonable doubt, and you only have to show that the doubt is reasonable. Then weigh the evidence piece by piece, and draw the inference that favors your client. Rebut the strongest point the other side made, never the weakest. Reiterate one clear theme, three or four times, until the jury can repeat it in the deliberation room. And close with a plea that is calm and human: what you ask of them is not sympathy, but a fair reading of the facts. It is the lawyer who keeps it simple who is most compelling.

You are listening to the legal affairs review. The trial of the State versus Andrade reached its closing arguments today, and the courtroom was full. In her summation, the prosecutor argued that the evidence was cumulative and compelling. But it was the defense that controlled the room. Not once did counsel raise his voice. What he did instead was weigh each fact aloud and ask the jury to draw a different inference. He reminded them that it is reasonable doubt, not certainty, that decides the case, and he undermined the central witness with a single quiet question. The jury will begin to deliberate tomorrow morning. Whatever the verdict, observers agreed that the closing was a model of restraint. We will follow the case as it develops.
